name: American Grotesk
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; a reprisal of Franklin Gothic, framed as a key part of American graphic vernacular history, particularly persuasive communication, drawn at a moment of contested authorship and authenticity.
summary: A reprisal of Franklin Gothic by Kris Sowersby, leaning into American vernacular grotesques for editorial and persuasive work.
description: A grotesque sans by Kris Sowersby that reworks Franklin Gothic, the typeface Klim describe as a fixture of American graphic vernacular and persuasive communication. Fourteen styles run across seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, and the family extends into American Grotesk Condensed and American Grotesk Compressed for tighter measures and posters. Sits in editorial, campaign and identity work where you want the warmth and rhythm of Franklin without its dated contours.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify American Grotesk as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2024. They describe it as a reprisal of Franklin Gothic, citing Franklin's role in American graphic vernacular history and persuasive communication, and frame the release against a present-day backdrop of contested truth and authorship.

name: American Grotesk Compressed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; the most compressed width in the American Grotesk system, rooted in Franklin Gothic and the wider American vernacular.
summary: The most compressed width of American Grotesk, built for posters and mastheads where every millimetre of horizontal space counts.
description: A compressed grotesque by Kris Sowersby that takes the American Grotesk system to its narrowest setting. Fourteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, and the family connects to the standard and Condensed widths so editorial systems can flex from body to billboard. The Woodtype stylistic set hints at the American poster heritage Klim describes for the wider family.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify American Grotesk Compressed as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2024. It is the compressed width within the American Grotesk system, sharing the family's Franklin Gothic reprisal and its grounding in American graphic vernacular and persuasive communication.

name: American Grotesk Condensed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; the condensed cut within the American Grotesk system, drawing on Franklin Gothic's role in American graphic vernacular and persuasive communication.
summary: The condensed cut of American Grotesk, holding Franklin Gothic's voice at tighter measures for headlines, posters and packaging.
description: A condensed grotesque by Kris Sowersby that compresses the American Grotesk system without losing its Franklin Gothic accent. Fourteen styles span Thin to Black with matching italics, sitting between the standard width and the tighter Compressed cut. Reach for it on posters, mastheads and packaging where you want vertical economy and the same vernacular American voice.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify American Grotesk Condensed as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2024. It is the condensed width within the American Grotesk system, sharing the family's reprisal of Franklin Gothic and its grounding in American graphic vernacular and persuasive communication.

name: Calibre
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Neo-grotesque; a neo-grotesk inspired by lettering on West Berlin street signs, taking rationality from Nebiolo's Recta and applying optical correction to the engineer's geometry.
summary: A crisp neo-grotesk drawn from West Berlin signage and Nebiolo's Recta, optically corrected for screens, identity and tech.
description: A neo-grotesque by Kris Sowersby drawn from lettering on West Berlin street signs, with the rationality of Nebiolo's Recta tempered by optical correction. Calibre was designed alongside Metric and shares its underlying geometry, but key letterforms take a more contemporary, crisp finish. Fourteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, which is why Calibre keeps showing up in tech, fintech and product brands wanting a calm, engineered sans without the chill of pure geometry.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Calibre as Modern: Lineal/Neo-grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2011. They describe it as a neo-grotesk inspired by West Berlin street signage, drawing rationality from Nebiolo's Recta and softening the engineer's geometry with typographic optical correction. Calibre was designed alongside Metric, sharing its fundamental geometry but differing in key letterforms.

name: Die Grotesk
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Neo-grotesque; Klim's first retail variable font, shaped in the long shadow of Helvetica and rooted in study of original metal cuts plus modernist masters, with optical and weight axes for typographic texture across sizes.
summary: Klim's first retail variable grotesk, drawn in Helvetica's shadow with four optical sizes from 6pt to 42pt.
description: A neo-grotesque by Kris Sowersby, Klim's first retail variable font, drawn in what Klim call the long shadow of Helvetica through study of the original metal cuts and modernist masters. The family runs across four optical sizes (A at 6pt, B at 18pt, C at 30pt, D at 42pt) and eight weights from Hairline to Heavy with italics, with variable axes for optical size and weight when you want continuous control. Klim pitch it as both homage and critique, made for typographic texture that holds from caption to billboard.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Die Grotesk as Modern: Lineal/Neo-grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2025. They describe it as Klim's first retail variable font, shaped in the long shadow of Helvetica and rooted in study of original metal cuts and modernist masters. Variable axes for optical size (6 to 42) and weight (100 to 900) sit alongside four static optical sizes for typographic texture across sizes.

name: Domaine Display
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Latin; a sharp, elegant serif blending French and British genres, with bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals; italics inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série.
summary: A sharp display serif with triangular serifs and hooked terminals, fit for fashion, luxury and editorial mastheads at large sizes.
description: A sharp display serif by Kris Sowersby that blends French and British genres, with bracketed triangular serifs, distinctive hooked terminals and italics inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série. Six weights run from Regular to Black, and the family extends into Domaine Display Narrow and Domaine Display Condensed, which hold their elegance at tight measures. The contemporary alternative to HTF Didot or Bodoni when a luxury, fashion or editorial brand wants the sharpness without the chill.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Domaine Display as Modern: Transitional/Latin, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2013. They describe it as a sharp serif blending French and British traditions, with bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals; the italics are drawn from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série.

name: Domaine Display Condensed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Latin; the condensed display cut of Domaine, with bracketed triangular serifs, hooked terminals and italics drawn from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série.
summary: A condensed display serif holding Domaine's triangular serifs and hooked terminals at tight measures for fashion and editorial mastheads.
description: A sharp condensed display serif by Kris Sowersby, the tightest cut in the Domaine Display system, with bracketed triangular serifs, hooked terminals and italics inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série. Twelve styles run from Regular to Black with matching italics, made to keep their elegance when set in narrow columns or stacked over images. Frequently paired with Domaine Text and Söhne in fashion, wine and editorial branding.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Domaine Display Condensed as Modern: Transitional/Latin, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2013. They describe Domaine as a sharp serif blending French and British genres, with bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals, and italics drawn from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série. This is the condensed display cut within the wider Domaine system.

name: Domaine Display Narrow
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Latin; the narrow display cut between the standard Display and the Condensed, sharing Domaine's triangular serifs, hooked terminals and Deberny & Peignot italics.
summary: A narrow display serif sitting between Domaine Display and Domaine Display Condensed, made for tight headlines that keep their elegance.
description: A narrow display serif by Kris Sowersby, the middle width within the Domaine Display system, with bracketed triangular serifs, hooked terminals and italics inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série. Twelve styles run from Regular to Black with matching italics, more economical than Domaine Display itself but less compressed than Domaine Display Condensed. A natural fit for fashion, wine and editorial mastheads where the columns are tight but the brand still wants air.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Domaine Display Narrow as Modern: Transitional/Latin, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2013. They describe Domaine as a sharp serif blending French and British genres, with bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals, and italics drawn from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série. This cut is the narrow display width within the Domaine system.

name: Domaine Sans Display
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque, High Contrast; the display cut of Domaine Sans, an exploration of contrast in sans serifs alongside Domaine's earlier work in the Latin serif genre.
summary: A high-contrast display sans by Kris Sowersby that explores contrast in sanserifs, made for fashion and editorial display.
description: A high-contrast display sans by Kris Sowersby, sitting at the largest size in the Domaine Sans system. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, with swashes and old-style or lining numerals available through OpenType. Klim describe Domaine Sans as an exploration of an unpopular genre, sans serifs with contrast, and at display sizes the stress and modulation read clearly, which is why fashion and editorial brands such as Vanity Fair's Hollywood issue have reached for it.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Domaine Sans Display as Lineal: Grotesque, High Contrast, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2014. They describe Domaine Sans as an exploration of an unpopular genre, sans serifs with contrast, following Domaine's earlier exploration of the Latin serif genre.

name: Domaine Sans Fine
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque, High Contrast; the finest optical cut of Domaine Sans, with the sharpest contrast, sharing the family's exploration of contrasted sans serifs.
summary: The sharpest optical cut of Domaine Sans, with the highest contrast for large-scale luxury and editorial display.
description: The Fine optical size of Domaine Sans by Kris Sowersby, drawn for the largest display sizes where the family's contrast can read at its sharpest. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics. Specimens for Domaine Sans were added to the Cooper Hewitt Collection in 2016, and the Fine cut tends to appear in luxury, fashion and high-end editorial work where the brief calls for the punch of a Didone but in a sans frame.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Domaine Sans Fine as Lineal: Grotesque, High Contrast, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2014. They describe Domaine Sans as an exploration of sans serifs with contrast, following Domaine's earlier exploration of the Latin serif genre. Fine is the highest-contrast optical size in the system.

name: Domaine Sans Text
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque, High Contrast; the text optical size of Domaine Sans, sharing the family's exploration of sans serifs with contrast and adding small caps for setting body copy.
summary: The text cut of Domaine Sans, with reduced contrast and small caps for body copy that still carries the family's editorial voice.
description: The Text optical size of Domaine Sans by Kris Sowersby, with the family's contrast tempered for body sizes and small caps added for editorial setting. Ten styles span Light to Black with matching italics, alongside small caps, all small caps, tabular figures and superiors. Reads with the warmth and authority of a serif text face while staying inside the sans genre, which is why it pairs neatly with Domaine Display, Domaine Text and Tiempos Text in editorial systems.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Domaine Sans Text as Lineal: Grotesque, High Contrast, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2014. They describe Domaine Sans as an exploration of sans serifs with contrast, following Domaine's earlier exploration of the Latin serif genre. Text is the optical size tuned for body copy.

name: Domaine Text
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Latin; the text cut of Domaine, with the family's bracketed triangular serifs, hooked terminals and Deberny & Peignot italics, with small caps and figure sets for editorial body copy.
summary: The text optical size of Domaine, tuned for body copy with small caps and figure sets while keeping the family's sharpness.
description: The Text optical size of Domaine by Kris Sowersby, where the family's bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals are recut for body sizes. Ten styles run Light to Black with matching italics drawn from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série, alongside small caps, all small caps and tabular figures. Pairs neatly with Domaine Display and Domaine Display Narrow in editorial, wine and luxury identities where you want the same voice from masthead to running text.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Domaine Text as Modern: Transitional/Latin, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2008. They describe Domaine as a sharp serif blending French and British genres, with bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals, and italics inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série. Text is the optical size cut for body copy.

name: Epicene Display
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Baroque Old-Style; inspired by 18th century punchcutters J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann, with exaggerated details for vigour at large sizes; Klim also frames the project around the idea that typefaces have no gender.
summary: A Baroque display serif drawn from Rosart and Fleischmann, with sharp details that read with vigour at large sizes.
description: A Baroque display serif by Kris Sowersby that draws on the 18th century punches of J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann, with exaggerated details engineered to add vigour at headline sizes. Five weights from Light to Black sit alongside ten italic styles, and Epicene Display partners with Epicene Text in the wider system. It suits editorial mastheads, fashion covers and cultural posters where a sharp, characterful serif is wanted without falling into Didone chill.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Epicene as a Baroque Old-Style, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2021. They describe it as inspired by the 18th century maestros J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann, with exaggerated details that add rigour at small sizes and vigour at large sizes; the Display cut sits alongside Epicene Text in the family.

name: Epicene Text
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Baroque Old-Style; the text counterpart to Epicene Display, drawing on Rosart and Fleischmann with details tuned to add rigour at small sizes.
summary: A Baroque text serif drawn from Rosart and Fleischmann, tuned with quieter detail for body copy in books and editorial.
description: A Baroque text serif by Kris Sowersby and the body-copy companion to Epicene Display, rooted in the same 18th century work of Rosart and Fleischmann. Five roman weights from Light to Black come with matching italics, with details settled down so the page reads steady at book and magazine sizes. Reaches for a literary register where Tiempos Text would feel too neutral and a Garalde revival would feel too quiet.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Epicene Text as a Baroque Old-Style, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2021. They pair it with Epicene Display in a single Baroque family inspired by J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann, with details tuned to add rigour at small sizes.

name: Family
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Oldstyle Serif; based on Clearface by Morris Fuller Benton and his father Linn Boyd Benton, keeping its easy-going curves and idiosyncrasies while updating it for present-day use.
summary: A warm old-style serif drawn from Benton's Clearface, updated as an everyday workhorse with twelve styles and a variable axis.
description: An old-style serif by Kris Sowersby that takes Clearface, drawn by Morris Fuller Benton and his father Linn Boyd Benton, and brings its easy-going curves and idiosyncrasies into present-day use. Six weights from Light to Black come with matching italics across twelve styles, alongside variable roman and italic axes covering 300 to 900. Reads warm and slightly informal, which suits editorial, brand and cultural work that wants character without the period drama of a strict revival.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Family as an Oldstyle Serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2023. They describe it as an everyday typeface based on Clearface, originally drawn by Morris Fuller and Linn Boyd Benton, with the lineage's easy-going curves and idiosyncrasies kept and updated for modern use.

name: Feijoa
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Garalde/Oldstyle; built on the principle that 'a straight line is a dead line', giving warm, curvaceous letterforms that humanise digital typography; ligatures inspired by Zuzana Licko's Mrs Eaves.
summary: Klim's first release: a warm humanist serif built on the idea that a straight line is a dead line, fit for book typography and reading.
description: A humanist serif by Kris Sowersby, the first typeface released by Klim Type Foundry, drawn from the principle that 'a straight line is a dead line'. The text weights, Medium, Italic and Bold, are built for book typography and extended reading, with a Display cut tuned for headlines at 16pt and larger. Several fanciful ligatures take their cue from Zuzana Licko's Mrs Eaves, giving the family a touch of bookish warmth that works well on long-form editorial and literary covers.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Feijoa as Modern: Garalde/Oldstyle, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2007 as the foundry's first typeface. They describe it as built on the principle that a straight line is a dead line, with warm, curvaceous letterforms that humanise digital type, and ligatures inspired by Zuzana Licko's Mrs Eaves.

name: Financier Display
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional Serif; drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign, with the display cut taking Perpetua's stately charm from Eric Gill's lineage.
summary: A British-feeling display serif drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign, with Perpetua's stately charm in the Gill lineage.
description: A transitional display serif by Kris Sowersby, drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign and released publicly in 2016. The aesthetic comes from Eric Gill's classic letterforms, with Financier Display taking Perpetua's stately charm and the italics reconciling mechanical sloped romans with traditional calligraphic forms at a narrow, slight incline. Six weights from Light to Black across twelve styles plus a variable roman and italic axis cover news mastheads, features and arts pages with a frugal, staccato grace.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Financier Display as Modern: Transitional Serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2016. They describe it as an elegant, authoritative serif with British heritage, drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign, with the Display cut taking Perpetua's stately charm from Eric Gill's lineage.

name: Financier Text
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Slab Serif; the text counterpart drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign, with pragmatic detailing taken from Solus and Joanna in the Gill lineage.
summary: A transitional text serif with slab leanings, drawn from Gill's Solus and Joanna for the 2014 Financial Times redesign.
description: A transitional text serif by Kris Sowersby, the body-copy companion to Financier Display drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign. Pragmatic detailing comes from Eric Gill's Solus and Joanna, giving it a slab leaning that holds shape on broadsheets and on narrow mobile screens. Four weights from Regular to Black across eight styles plus variable roman and italic axes carry features, news and longform across the FT's wider editorial system.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Financier Text as Modern: Transitional/Slab Serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2016. They describe it as the text counterpart to Financier Display, drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign, with pragmatic detailing taken from Solus and Joanna in Eric Gill's lineage.

name: Founders Grotesk
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; an amalgamation of classic grotesks drawing on Millar & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric series, and the tight spacing of Helvetica's Halbfett (Medium) headline metal cuts from the late 1950s.
summary: A grotesk family rooted in early 20th century British models and Helvetica's Halbfett spacing, made for modern editorial and brand work.
description: A grotesk by Kris Sowersby that pulls rudimentary geometry and serpentine curves from Millar & Richard's Grotesque series, details from H. W. Caslon's Doric, and tight spacing strategies from Helvetica's Halbfett (Medium) headline metal cuts. Five upright weights run from Light to Bold across ten styles, and the wider system extends into Founders Grotesk Text, Mono, Condensed and X-Condensed. Common in editorial, fashion and brand identity when the brief calls for a slightly warmer, less corporate alternative to Helvetica.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Founders Grotesk as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2010. They describe it as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks, drawing rudimentary geometry from Millar & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series, details from H. W. Caslon's Doric, and tight spacing from Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts of the late 1950s.

name: Founders Grotesk Condensed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; the condensed cut of Founders Grotesk, an amalgamation of Miller & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts.
summary: A condensed grotesque drawn from Miller & Richard, Caslon Doric and Helvetica headline cuts, built for tight headlines and posters.
description: The condensed cut of Founders Grotesk, by Kris Sowersby, pulling rudimentary geometry and serpentine curves from Miller & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series, further details from H. W. Caslon's Doric series, and tight spacing strategies from Helvetica's Halbfett headline-sized metal cuts of the late 1950s. Five weights from Light to Bold give posters, mastheads and packaging a confident, editorial-feeling condensed sans without the rigidity of a strict revival. Sits within the wider Founders Grotesk system alongside the standard, Text, X-Condensed and Mono cuts.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Founders Grotesk Condensed as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2011. They describe Founders Grotesk as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks, drawing on Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric and the tight spacing of Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts from the late 1950s.

name: Founders Grotesk Mono
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque Monospaced; the monospaced cut of Founders Grotesk, sharing the family's roots in Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts.
summary: A grotesque monospace inside the Founders Grotesk system, fit for code, editorial detail and brand pairings.
description: The monospaced cut of Founders Grotesk, by Kris Sowersby, sharing the lineage of Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts. Five weights from Light to Bold sit alongside the parent Founders Grotesk, Text, Condensed and X-Condensed cuts, with a slashed zero, fractions and capital forms in the OpenType set. Used widely in editorial captions, code blocks and brand systems where the mono needs to hold a conversation with a wider grotesque family.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Founders Grotesk Mono as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque Monospaced, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2014. They describe the parent Founders Grotesk as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks drawing on Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett, with the Mono extending those forms onto a fixed pitch.

name: Founders Grotesk Text
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; the text-sized cut of Founders Grotesk, with the same lineage as the wider family but tuned for body copy.
summary: The text cut of Founders Grotesk, with five weights and italics tuned for body copy in editorial and brand systems.
description: The text cut of Founders Grotesk, by Kris Sowersby, redrawn from the parent grotesque so it holds shape at body sizes. Five weights from Light to Bold across ten styles include true italics, with the same lineage of Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts. Used as the workhorse text companion in brand and editorial systems where Founders Grotesk runs the headlines, with Condensed, X-Condensed and Mono extending the family.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Founders Grotesk Text as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2013. They describe Founders Grotesk as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks drawing on Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts, with the Text cut tuned for body copy.

name: Founders Grotesk X-Condensed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; the extra-condensed cut of Founders Grotesk, sharing the family's roots in Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett.
summary: An extra-condensed grotesque inside the Founders Grotesk system, drawn for posters, mastheads and tight vertical headlines.
description: The extra-condensed cut of Founders Grotesk, by Kris Sowersby, narrower again than the Condensed and tuned for posters, mastheads and stacked headlines. Five weights from Light to Bold sit beside the standard Founders Grotesk, Text, Condensed and Mono cuts, sharing the same lineage of Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett headline metal cuts. The cut culture studios reach for when a grotesque has to set a long word in a short column without thinning the weight.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Founders Grotesk X-Condensed as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2011. They describe Founders Grotesk as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks drawing on Miller & Richard's Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts, with the X-Condensed cut taking those forms to an extra-narrow proportion.

name: Geograph
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Geometric; originally designed for National Geographic, drawing on the idealism of Futura and the pragmatism of Super Grotesk, with stylistic sets that toggle between Futura alternates and blunt alternates.
summary: A geometric sans built for National Geographic, blending Futura idealism with Super Grotesk pragmatism and a deep set of alternates.
description: A geometric sans by Kris Sowersby originally drawn for National Geographic, sitting between the idealism of Futura and the pragmatism of Super Grotesk. Twelve styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics and a variable-weight axis, and stylistic sets swap in Futura alternates (single-storey a, e, g) or blunted A, M, N, V, W. A workable choice when a brand wants the geometric look without locking itself to one historical reference.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Geograph as Modern: Lineal/Geometric, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2019. They describe it as a contemporary geometric sans serif originally designed for National Geographic, drawing on the idealism of Futura and the pragmatism of Super Grotesk, with a suite of alternate letterforms for flexible texture and tone.

name: Heldane Display
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Classical: Garalde/Oldstyle; a renaissance-inspired serif that amalgamates details from Hendrik van den Keere, Claude Garamont, Robert Granjon and Simon de Colines into a cohesive whole, with a deep set of italic swashes.
summary: A renaissance-inspired display garalde drawing on Garamont, Granjon, van den Keere and de Colines, with rich swash italics for headlines.
description: A display garalde by Kris Sowersby that pulls details from the renaissance work of Hendrik van den Keere, Claude Garamont, Robert Granjon and Simon de Colines rather than copying any single source. Six styles run Regular, Medium and Bold with italics that carry an extensive swash programme (e, h, k, nm, y, z and caps). Sits at the display end of the Heldane system alongside Heldane Text, and reads well on editorial covers, luxury identities and book jackets.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Heldane Display as Classical: Garalde/Oldstyle, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2018. They describe Heldane as a contemporary serif inspired by the renaissance works of Hendrik van den Keere, Claude Garamont, Robert Granjon and Simon de Colines, amalgamating their best details into one cohesive whole.

name: Heldane Text
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Classical: Garalde/Oldstyle; a contemporary serif drawing on Hendrik van den Keere, Claude Garamont, Robert Granjon and Simon de Colines, with old-style numerals, small caps and a full italic swash programme tuned for body text.
summary: A contemporary garalde text serif blending Garamont, Granjon, van den Keere and de Colines, sturdy enough to carry long-form body copy.
description: A garalde text serif by Kris Sowersby drawn from the renaissance work of Hendrik van den Keere, Claude Garamont, Robert Granjon and Simon de Colines, refined through digital drawing for present-day setting. Six styles run Regular, Medium and Bold with italics, plus small caps, old-style and tabular numerals, and a swash programme inherited from the Display cut. The Text size in the Heldane system, paired naturally with Heldane Display for headlines.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Heldane Text as Classical: Garalde/Oldstyle, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2018. They describe Heldane as a contemporary serif inspired by the renaissance works of Hendrik van den Keere, Claude Garamont, Robert Granjon and Simon de Colines, amalgamating their best details rather than emulating any single typeface.

name: Karbon
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Geometric; an open geometric sans exploring Paul Renner's reductionist Futura concept through the proportions of Eric Gill's eponymous sans, with a slight nod to Jan Tschichold's Uhertype sans-serif.
summary: An open geometric sans crossing Futura's reductionism with Gill Sans proportions, with a Hairline-to-Bold range tuned for display and text.
description: A geometric sans by Kris Sowersby that runs Paul Renner's reductionist Futura concept through the proportions of Eric Gill's eponymous sans, with a side glance at Jan Tschichold's Uhertype. Fourteen styles span Hairline to Bold with matching italics, giving a wide tonal range from light display setting to functional text. Often picked when a brand wants geometric clarity without Futura's exact silhouette, and pairs naturally with Karbon Slab Stencil for display lockups.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Karbon as Modern: Lineal/Geometric, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2009. They describe it as an open, geometric sans serif with a contemporary spartan finish, exploring Paul Renner's reductionist Futura concept through the proportions of Eric Gill's eponymous sans, with a slight nod to Jan Tschichold's Uhertype.

name: Karbon Slab Stencil
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Mechanistic/Stencil; extends Karbon's spartan geometric linework into slab-serif territory, with a logical programme of breaks referencing the industrial heritage of stencilled letterforms.
summary: Karbon's geometric skeleton pushed into slab-serif territory with stencilled breaks, fit for industrial and utility-flavoured display work.
description: A stencil slab by Kris Sowersby that takes the spartan geometry of Karbon and adds slab serifs plus a logical pattern of breaks, citing the industrial heritage of stencilled letterforms. Two styles only, Regular and Bold, with no italic. Sits as a display companion to Karbon proper, useful for posters, packaging and signage that wants a mechanical, slightly utilitarian voice.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Karbon Slab Stencil as Modern: Mechanistic/Stencil, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2009. They describe it as an extension of Karbon's spartan linework into slab-serif territory, with a logical programme of breaks referencing the industrial heritage of stencilled letterforms.

name: Maelstrom
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as a Reversed-Stress Slab Serif; razor-thin spacing forms narrow strips of light through the black mass. Sits in the Maelstrom collection alongside the sans-serif Maelstrom Sans.
summary: A single-weight reversed-stress slab serif with razor-thin spacing, leaning into a perverse Italian-genre charm for display sizes.
description: A reversed-stress slab by Kris Sowersby with weight pushed onto the horizontals and razor-thin spacing forming narrow strips of light through the black mass. Released as a single Bold cut, no italic. The sister face to Maelstrom Sans inside the Maelstrom collection, and a deliberate engagement with what Klim describe as a perpetual typographic outsider, never fashionable yet never abandoned.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Maelstrom as a Reversed-Stress Slab Serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2014. They describe Maelstrom and Maelstrom Sans as deliberately perverse reversed-stress typefaces, with Maelstrom's razor-thin spacing forming striking narrow strips of light through the black mass.

name: Maelstrom Sans
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as a Reversed-Stress Sans Serif; an uneasy mix of heavy horizontals and spindly diagonals that feels fragile, like it could splinter at any moment. Sits in the Maelstrom collection alongside the slab-serif Maelstrom.
summary: A single-weight reversed-stress display sans of heavy horizontals and spindly diagonals, made for posters and identity rather than text.
description: A reversed-stress display sans by Kris Sowersby that flips weight onto the horizontals and starves the diagonals, giving it a fragile, almost splintering quality. Released as a single Bold cut, no italic, no other widths. Sits in the Maelstrom collection alongside the slab-serif Maelstrom and belongs to the genre Klim describe as a perpetual typographic outsider, never fashionable yet never abandoned.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Maelstrom Sans as a Reversed-Stress Sans Serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2019. They describe Maelstrom and Maelstrom Sans as deliberately perverse reversed-stress typefaces, with Maelstrom Sans's uneasy mix of heavy horizontals and spindly diagonals making it feel fragile, like it could splinter at any moment.

name: Martina Plantijn
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Serif Oldstyle; a better Plantin, informed by Frank Hinman Pierpont's typeface and Pierpont's research of 16th century type at the Plantin-Moretus Museum in Antwerp, with decisive digital updates across roman and italic.
summary: A reworked Plantin tuned for present-day editorial work, with five weights of roman and italic and decisive digital corrections.
description: An old-style serif by Kris Sowersby that returns to Frank Hinman Pierpont's Plantin and the 16th century material Pierpont studied at the Plantin-Moretus Museum in Antwerp. Five weights run from Light to Black, each with a paired italic, and the drawing makes decisive digital updates rather than a faithful revival. Reads warm at body size and steady at display, so it sits naturally in books, magazines and editorial brands that want Plantin's character without its rougher legacy artefacts.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Martina Plantijn as Serif Oldstyle, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2023. They describe it as a better Plantin, informed by Frank Hinman Pierpont's typeface and his research of 16th century type at the Plantin-Moretus Museum in Antwerp, with decisive digital updates across the roman and italic cuts.

name: Metric
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Geometric/Humanist; a geometric humanist sans inspired by lettering on West Berlin street signs, with monoline construction tempered by typographic optical correction; designed alongside Calibre and sharing its fundamental geometry.
summary: A geometric humanist sans drawn from West Berlin street signs, monoline and rational but warmed by optical corrections.
description: A geometric humanist sans by Kris Sowersby, drawn from lettering on West Berlin street signs and softened with typographic optical correction. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each with an italic, giving it the range to handle signage, interface text and editorial setting. Designed alongside Calibre as a sibling system: the two share a fundamental geometry but diverge in how key letterforms finish, so Metric reads slightly more engineered while Calibre stays a touch more humanist.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Metric as Modern: Lineal/Geometric/Humanist, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2011. They describe it as a geometric humanist sans inspired by lettering on West Berlin street signs, with monoline construction tempered by typographic optical correction. It was designed alongside Calibre, sharing the same fundamental geometry.

name: Mānuka
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes that pilfers deviant details from Teutonic timber type and grafts a contemporary antipodean aesthetic onto 19th century German root-stock.
summary: A compressed display family riffing on 19th century German timber type, drawn for poster sizes with tight spacing and sharp joins.
description: A compressed display by Kris Sowersby that pulls deviant details from Teutonic timber type and grafts them onto a contemporary antipodean voice. Seven weights run from Thin through to Ultra, with closed apertures, tight spacing and sharp joins that build a dense texture at poster sizes. The wider Mānuka collection extends into Mānuka Condensed and Mānuka Slab, so the system carries one mood across grotesque and slab forms.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Mānuka as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2021. They describe it as a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes, pilfering deviant details from Teutonic timber type and grafting a contemporary antipodean aesthetic onto 19th century German root-stock.

name: Mānuka Condensed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes, with deviant details pilfered from Teutonic timber type, grafting a contemporary antipodean aesthetic onto 19th century German root-stock.
summary: A compressed grotesque cut from Teutonic wood-type roots, running Thin to Ultra for posters, packaging and bold display lockups.
description: A compressed display grotesque by Kris Sowersby with sharp joins, closed apertures and tight spacing, pulling deviant details from 19th century Teutonic timber type. Seven upright weights run from Thin to Ultra, no italics. Sits in the Mānuka collection alongside Mānuka Slab, and works hardest at very large sizes where the compressed texture reads, in Klim's words, like sunlight sparkling through a forest canopy.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Mānuka Condensed as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2021. They describe Mānuka as new growth from old wood, a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes, grafting a contemporary antipodean aesthetic onto 19th century German timber-type root-stock.

name: Mānuka Slab
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Slab-Serif; the slab cut of the Mānuka collection, sharing compressed proportions and Teutonic timber-type details with Mānuka Condensed.
summary: The slab partner to Mānuka Condensed, compressed and rooted in Teutonic timber type, running Thin to Ultra for display work.
description: A compressed slab by Kris Sowersby that takes Mānuka's antipodean reading of 19th century Teutonic timber type and adds slab serifs. Seven upright weights run from Thin to Ultra with stylistic alternates for a and R, no italics. Pairs naturally with Mānuka Condensed inside the same collection, and earns its keep on posters, packaging and editorial display where the texture needs to feel hand-cut rather than digital.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Mānuka Slab as Modern: Slab-Serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2021. They describe Mānuka as new growth from old wood, a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes, with deviant details pilfered from Teutonic timber type and grafted onto a contemporary antipodean aesthetic.

name: National
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; a deceptively simple sans drawn as a stylistic opposite to Helvetica, with looser spacing, unambiguous forms, old-style figures and a true italic, drawing details from the era of Akzidenz Grotesk.
summary: An Akzidenz-era grotesque drawn as the stylistic opposite of Helvetica, with looser spacing and a true italic for long-form reading.
description: An Akzidenz-rooted grotesque by Kris Sowersby, drawn as a stylistic opposite to Helvetica with looser spacing, unambiguous forms, old-style figures and a genuine italic rather than a sloped roman. Eighteen styles run from Thin to Black with paired italics, aimed at long-form reading at small sizes while still holding up at display. Klim now also offers National 2, the 10-year remaster, but the original National stays in the library for projects that want the earlier drawing and its specific tone of voice.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify National as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2007. They describe it as a deceptively simple sans serif drawn as a stylistic opposite to Helvetica, with looser spacing, unambiguous forms, old-style figures and a true italic, with details drawn from the era of Akzidenz Grotesk.

name: National 2
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; a complete 10-year overhaul of National with every letter redrawn and remastered, better weight distribution, refined curves and three new widths added (Narrow, Condensed, Compressed).
summary: A redrawn Akzidenz-era grotesque sans, ten years in the making, with refined curves and a four-width family for any brief.
description: A redrawn grotesque by Kris Sowersby, ten years in the making, with every letter remastered, weight distribution corrected and curves refined. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, and the wider system extends into National 2 Narrow, Condensed and Compressed for tighter settings. The Akzidenz-era detailing keeps the voice humble and workmanlike, which is why brands and editorial teams reach for it when they want a steady sans without obvious mannerisms.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify National 2 as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2017. They describe it as a complete overhaul of their popular sans National, ten years in the making, with every letter redrawn and remastered and three new widths (Narrow, Condensed and Compressed) added. The details come from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a humble, workmanlike character.

name: National 2 Compressed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; the tightest of three new widths added in the 10-year overhaul of National, drawing details from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk for a humble, workmanlike character.
summary: The tightest width in the National 2 system, an Akzidenz-rooted grotesque drawn for headlines and dense column setting.
description: The compressed cut of National 2 by Kris Sowersby, the tightest of three new widths added in the 10-year overhaul of National. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, all redrawn and remastered with refined curves and better weight distribution. Sits inside the wider National 2 system alongside National 2, Narrow and Condensed, so the same Akzidenz-era voice carries from running text into very tight headlines.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify National 2 Compressed as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2017. They describe National 2 as a complete overhaul of National, ten years in the making, with every letter redrawn and three new widths (Narrow, Condensed and Compressed). The details are drawn from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a humble, workmanlike character.

name: National 2 Condensed
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; one of three new widths in the 10-year overhaul of National, sitting between Narrow and Compressed, with details drawn from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk.
summary: The middle condensed width of the National 2 system, a redrawn Akzidenz-rooted grotesque for tight columns and headlines.
description: The condensed cut of National 2 by Kris Sowersby, sitting between Narrow and Compressed in the redrawn National 2 system. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with paired italics, with every letter remastered, refined curves and improved weight distribution. The Akzidenz-era detailing keeps the voice humble and workmanlike at small sizes, while the condensed proportions earn their place in editorial headlines, social design and wayfinding.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify National 2 Condensed as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2017. They describe National 2 as a complete overhaul of National, ten years in the making, with every letter redrawn and three new widths (Narrow, Condensed and Compressed) added. The details come from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving the family a humble, workmanlike character.

name: National 2 Narrow
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque; the widest of the three new condensed widths added in the 10-year overhaul of National, drawing on Akzidenz-Grotesk era detailing.
summary: The narrow cut of National 2, a gently tightened Akzidenz-rooted grotesque for editorial and brand work where space is short.
description: The narrow cut of National 2 by Kris Sowersby, the widest of the three condensed widths added in the redrawn National 2 system. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with paired italics, redrawn and remastered with cleaner curves and better weight distribution. Sits well in editorial and brand work that needs a slightly tighter sans without going as compact as Condensed or Compressed, while keeping the family's Akzidenz-era detailing intact.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify National 2 Narrow as Modern: Lineal/Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2017. They describe National 2 as a complete overhaul of National, ten years in the making, with every letter redrawn and three new widths (Narrow, Condensed and Compressed) added. Its details are drawn from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a humble, workmanlike character.

name: Newzald
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Realist; a hardworking text serif for the international editorial environment, drawing its economical rigour from classic Dutch typefaces by J.F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischman, with a large x-height and slightly condensed forms.
summary: A hardworking Dutch-rooted text serif with a large x-height and tight forms, drawn for international editorial setting.
description: A transitional text serif by Kris Sowersby drawing on classic Dutch types by J.F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischman, with a large x-height and slightly condensed forms that fit many words to a column without feeling cramped. Four weights run from Book through Medium and Bold to an emphatic Black, each with a paired italic. Sits well in editorial and longform settings where the serif needs to be economical, legible and quietly characterful at body size.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Newzald as Modern: Transitional/Realist, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2008. They describe it as a hardworking text typeface for the international editorial environment, with economical rigour drawn from classic Dutch typefaces by J.F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischman, a large x-height and slightly condensed forms.

name: Pitch
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Moderns: Mechanistic/Slab Serif Monospace; described as a love letter to the typewriter, a synthesis of digital and analogue that evokes typewritten text rather than emulating it.
summary: A slab serif monospace by Kris Sowersby that channels typewriter character without copying its quirks, suited to editorial and code settings.
description: A monospaced slab serif that reads as a considered nod to typewriter typography rather than a literal revival. Five weights from Light to Bold each carry a true italic, drawn so the slab terminals stay calm at body sizes. Reach for it when an editorial or technical brand wants the rhythm and texture of fixed-width letters without the flat sterility of a standard coding mono.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Pitch as Moderns: Mechanistic/Slab Serif Monospace, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2012. They describe it as a love letter to the typewriter, a synthesis of digital and analogue that evokes typewritten text rather than emulating it.

name: Pitch Sans
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Sans Serif Monospace; a typewriter-inspired sans monospace, sibling to Pitch, described as a synthesis of digital and analogue that evokes typewritten text rather than emulating it.
summary: A sans monospace that evokes typewritten text without imitating it, sibling to the slab-serif Pitch.
description: A monospaced sans by Kris Sowersby, the sans companion to Pitch, drawn as a love letter to the typewriter rather than a literal revival. Five weights run from Light to Bold with paired italics, all on the same fixed advance width, so it locks into code, captions and tabular settings cleanly. Sits comfortably in editorial and tech work that wants a typewriter atmosphere without the worn-ribbon pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Pitch Sans as Modern: Lineal/Sans Serif Monospace, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2018. They describe Pitch as a love letter to the typewriter, a synthesis of digital and analogue that evokes typewritten text rather than emulating it. Pitch Sans is the sans-serif sibling within the wider Pitch collection.

name: Signifier
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Oldstyle; a Brutalist response to 17th century typefaces, recasting the lead, antimony and tin of the Fell Types into Bezier curves and sharp vectors determined by machine logic.
summary: A Brutalist response to 17th century Fell Types, with sharp vectors and machine-cut precision across seven weights with italics.
description: An old-style serif by Kris Sowersby that takes the Oxford Fell Types as a starting point and recasts their lead-and-antimony texture as deliberately sharp digital vectors. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, all with italics, and a variable weight axis is supplied with the full family. Sits well in art, fashion and editorial work where a serif needs to feel both historic and unmistakably contemporary.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Signifier as Modern: Oldstyle, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2020. They describe it as a Brutalist response to 17th century typefaces, recasting the Fell Types' lead, antimony and tin into Bezier curves and sharp vectors driven by machine logic.

name: Söhne
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; described as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk framed through the reality of Helvetica, drawing on the Standard Medium used in Unimark's NYC Subway wayfinding.
summary: A grotesque sans rooted in Akzidenz and Standard Medium, balancing analogue warmth with quiet authority for editorial and tech brands.
description: A neo-grotesque by Kris Sowersby drawn from the Standard Medium signage on Unimark's New York City Subway wayfinding and the wider Akzidenz-Grotesk lineage, with apertures slightly more open than Helvetica's and joints subtly thinned. Eight weights run from Extraleicht to Extrafett with Buch and Kräftig as the workhorse text cuts, and the system extends into Söhne Breit (wide), Söhne Schmal (condensed) and Söhne Mono. Used widely across editorial, tech and fashion when the brief calls for precise neutrality without sterility.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Söhne as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2019. They describe it as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk seen through the reality of Helvetica, drawing on the Standard Medium used in Unimark's New York City Subway wayfinding programme.

name: Söhne Breit
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; the wide cut in the Söhne system, capturing the analogue materiality of Standard Medium used in Unimark's NYC Subway wayfinding.
summary: The wide cut of Söhne, holding the family's Akzidenz-meets-Helvetica neutrality at expansive proportions for display and branding.
description: The wide companion to Söhne, drawn by Kris Sowersby with the same Akzidenz-Grotesk and Standard Medium reference points stretched horizontally. Eight weights from Extraleicht to Extrafett run with matching Kursiv italics, giving the same German weight nomenclature as the parent family. Brands reach for it when headlines need width and air without tipping into a true display face, often paired back with Söhne or Söhne Mono for body text.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Söhne Breit as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2019. They describe Söhne as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk seen through the reality of Helvetica, capturing the analogue materiality of Standard Medium used in Unimark's NYC Subway wayfinding.

name: Söhne Mono
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque, Monospaced; the fixed-width cut in the Söhne system, sharing the Akzidenz-Grotesk lineage of the parent family.
summary: A grotesque monospace tied to the Söhne system, giving fixed-width text the same Akzidenz lineage as its proportional sibling.
description: The monospaced cut in the Söhne system by Kris Sowersby, sharing the parent family's Akzidenz-Grotesk reference and Standard Medium tone. Eight weights with Kursiv italics give a fuller weight ladder than most code fonts offer, and stylistic alternates handle the alternate a and g for designers wanting to soften the grotesque feel. Used for code, data UI and editorial captions where the rest of the system already runs on Söhne.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Söhne Mono as Lineal: Grotesque, Monospaced, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2019. They describe Söhne as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk seen through the reality of Helvetica, capturing the analogue materiality of Standard Medium used in Unimark's NYC Subway wayfinding.

name: Söhne Schmal
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Lineal: Grotesque; the condensed cut in the Söhne system, drawn from the same Akzidenz-Grotesk and Standard Medium reference points as the parent family.
summary: The condensed cut of Söhne, taking the Akzidenz lineage into tight columns and stacked headlines without losing the family's neutrality.
description: The narrow companion to Söhne, drawn by Kris Sowersby for measures and stacked headlines that the standard width can't hold. Eight weights from Extraleicht to Extrafett run with Kursiv italics, keeping the Söhne family's eight-step German nomenclature consistent across widths. Editorial mastheads, posters and tight UI labels are the natural home, often paired with Söhne for body copy.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Söhne Schmal as Lineal: Grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2019. They describe Söhne as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk seen through the reality of Helvetica, capturing the analogue materiality of Standard Medium used in Unimark's NYC Subway wayfinding.

name: The Future
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Geometric sans-serif; a homage to Futura drawn from Paul Renner's original production drawings, articulating his musings on modernity, abstraction and purity.
summary: A Futura homage drawn from Renner's production drawings, with seven weights, true italics and a deep set of avant-garde alternates.
description: A geometric sans by Kris Sowersby, built from Paul Renner's original production drawings rather than the many later interpretations of Futura. Seven weights from Thin to Black run with italics, and the stylistic sets carry the avant-garde alternate forms (square m, n, r, alt a and g) that Renner sketched but didn't ship. Sits alongside The Future Mono in the same family, and works for editorial, modernist branding and exhibition design where Futura's shape is the brief but the historical baggage isn't.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify The Future as Geometric sans-serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2022. They describe it as a homage to Futura, drawn by careful observation of Paul Renner's geometric architecture and avant-garde alternates from his original production drawings.

name: The Future Mono
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Monospaced geometric sans-serif; a speculative reimagining of Futura as a typewriter font commissioned by Kyota Sugimoto, sitting alongside The Future as a homage to Paul Renner's geometric classic.
summary: A geometric monospace by Kris Sowersby that imagines Futura as a typewriter face, with seven weights and a deep stylistic alternates set.
description: A geometric monospace by Kris Sowersby drawn as a speculative pairing for The Future, treating Futura as if it had been cut for a typewriter rather than for advertising. Seven weights from Thin to Black run with italics, and the OpenType set carries fifteen stylistic sets covering alt a, g, h, m, n, r, u and square forms borrowed from Renner's avant-garde alternates. Reads cleanly in code and captions, then turns expressive when the alternates are switched on for posters or art direction.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify The Future Mono as Monospaced geometric sans-serif, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2022. They describe it as a speculative take that reimagines Futura as a typewriter font commissioned by Kyota Sugimoto, alongside The Future, their homage to Paul Renner's progressive classic.

name: Tiempos Fine
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Realist; the sharpest optical size in the Tiempos system, keeping the proportions and letterfit of Tiempos Headline while intensifying the interplay between warm curves and sharp details.
summary: The sharpest optical cut in the Tiempos system, with high contrast and crisp terminals tuned for large editorial and fashion headlines.
description: The display optical size in the Tiempos system, drawn by Kris Sowersby and Noe Blanco. Six weights from Light to Black with italics keep the proportions and letterfit of Tiempos Headline, then push contrast and terminal sharpness for use at the largest sizes. A weight-axis variable font is supplied with the full family. Editorial covers, fashion mastheads and luxury display work where Tiempos Headline starts to feel slightly soft.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Tiempos Fine as Modern: Transitional/Realist, designed by Kris Sowersby and Noe Blanco and released in 2018. They describe it as the Fine size in the Tiempos system, keeping the proportions and letterfit of Tiempos Headline while intensifying the interplay between warm curves and sharp details.

name: Tiempos Headline
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Realist; the headline optical size in the Tiempos system, designed for larger sizes and striking a balance between practicality and elegance.
summary: The headline cut of Tiempos, balancing practicality and elegance for mastheads, news headlines and editorial display.
description: The headline optical size in the Tiempos system by Kris Sowersby, drawn from the Plantin and Times lineage that Tiempos Text reworks for body. Six weights from Light to Black run with italics, with a variable weight axis supplied with the full family. Quietly become a default for news and editorial mastheads (notably across major publications) when the brief asks for authority without rhetoric, and pairs cleanly with Söhne or Untitled Sans.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Tiempos Headline as Modern: Transitional/Realist, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2010. They describe it as the Headline size in the Tiempos system, designed for larger sizes and striking a balance between practicality and elegance.

name: Tiempos Text
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Transitional/Realist; takes the functionality of Plantin and Times and updates it for contemporary, economical typesetting.
summary: A workhorse text serif drawing on Plantin and Times, tuned for legible, economical body copy in editorial and news settings.
description: A pragmatic transitional serif by Kris Sowersby that updates the Plantin and Times News Roman lineage for contemporary, economical typesetting. The Tiempos system runs across three optical sizes (Text, Headline, Fine), so the same family carries you from body copy to mastheads without breaking voice. Reads quiet at small sizes and steady at large, which is why news, longform and editorial brands reach for it when the serif needs to stay out of the way.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Tiempos Text as Modern: Transitional/Realist, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2010. They describe it as Plantin and Times reimagined for present-day, space-efficient text setting, sitting at the Text size in the wider Tiempos system alongside Headline and Fine.

name: Untitled Sans
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Lineal/Neo-grotesque; a plain neogrotesk sans validated by the ideas of Jasper Morrison and Naoto Fukasawa's Super Normal project, with a deliberate aesthetic of not being designed by anyone in particular.
summary: A plain neo-grotesque drawn around Morrison and Fukasawa's Super Normal idea, deliberately ordinary and wearable across editorial and branding.
description: A neo-grotesque sans by Kris Sowersby drawn around the Super Normal project of Jasper Morrison and Naoto Fukasawa, deliberately resisting personality at the level of the individual letter. Five weights from Light to Black run with italics, and a variable weight axis is supplied with the full family. Pairs by concept (rather than skeleton) with Untitled Serif, and gets used widely by brands wanting a sans that recedes into the design system rather than performing.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Untitled Sans as Modern: Lineal/Neo-grotesque, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2017. They describe it as a plain neogrotesk sans validated by the ideas of Jasper Morrison and Naoto Fukasawa's Super Normal project, with a deliberate aesthetic of not being designed by anyone in particular.

name: Untitled Serif
classification_notes: Klim classifies it as Modern: Garalde/Oldstyle; drawn from the post-Caslon, pre-Times workhorses of metal type, sharing Untitled Sans's Super Normal concept rather than a shared skeleton.
summary: A quotidian old-style serif drawn from post-Caslon metal workhorses, paired by concept with Untitled Sans for plain, readable text.
description: An old-style serif by Kris Sowersby drawn from the post-Caslon, pre-Times workhorses that nearly every metal foundry once offered, then quietened in the spirit of Morrison and Fukasawa's Super Normal. Three weights (Regular, Medium, Bold) with italics keep the family lean, and a variable weight axis is supplied with the full family. Pairs by concept with Untitled Sans, and works for editorial body, books and brands that want a serif with no obvious authorial signature.
foundry_blurb: Klim classify Untitled Serif as Modern: Garalde/Oldstyle, designed by Kris Sowersby and released in 2017. They describe it as drawn from the post-Caslon, pre-Times workhorses offered by almost every metal foundry of the time, paired with Untitled Sans by concept rather than skeleton, with a deliberate aesthetic of not being designed by anyone in particular.
